title: STANDARD FOR CREAM AND PREPARED CREAMS CXS 288-1976

sections.section: STANDARD FOR CREAM AND PREPARED CREAMS CXS 288-1976
sections.summary: Title of the standard: "Standard for Cream and Prepared Creams (CXS 288-1976)."

sections.section: Formerly CODEX STAN A-9-1976. Adopted in 1976. Revised in 2003, 2008. Amended in 2010, 2018, 2022.
sections.summary: Revision history: formerly CODEX STAN A-9-1976, adopted 1976, revised 2003 and 2008, and amended in 2010, 2018 and 2022.

sections.section: ## 2022 Amendment The following amendment was made to the text of the standard following decisions taken at the forty-fifth session of the Codex Alimentarius Commission in December 2022.
sections.summary: Amendment notice (2022): text of the standard was amended following CAC decisions, replacing previous non-retail container labelling text so that labelling of non-retail containers should follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).
Scope: the standard applies to cream and prepared creams for direct consumption or further processing as defined in Section 2.

Description (Section 2) defines cream, reconstituted and recombined cream, and prepared creams and subcategories including pre-packaged liquid cream, whipping cream, cream packed under pressure, whipped cream, fermented cream and acidified cream, with notes on processing and claims for viable microorganisms where relevant.

sections.section: 2.1 Cream is the fluid 1  milk product comparatively rich in fat, in the form of an emulsion of fat-in-skimmed milk, obtained by physical separation from milk.
sections.summary: Definition 2.1: cream is defined as the fluid milk product relatively rich in fat, an emulsion of fat-in-skimmed milk obtained by physical separation from milk.

sections.section: 2.2 Reconstituted cream is cream obtained by reconstituting milk products with or without the addition of potable water and with the same end product characteristics as the product described in Section 2.1.
sections.summary: Defines 'Reconstituted cream' as cream obtained by reconstituting milk products, with or without potable water, to yield the same end-product characteristics as the cream described in Section 2.1.

sections.section: 2.3 Recombined cream is cream obtained by recombining milk products with or without the addition of potable water and with the same end product characteristics as the product described in Section 2.1.
sections.summary: Defines 'Recombined cream' as cream obtained by recombining milk products, with or without potable water, to yield the same end-product characteristics as the cream described in Section 2.1.

sections.section: 2.4 Prepared creams are the milk products obtained by subjecting cream, reconstituted cream and/or recombined cream to suitable treatments and processes to obtain the characteristic properties as specified below.
sections.summary: Defines 'Prepared creams' as cream, reconstituted or recombined cream subjected to suitable treatments to obtain specified properties and enumerates types (pre‑packaged liquid cream, whipping cream, cream packed under pressure, whipped cream, fermented cream—with requirements for viable starter cultures when claimed unless heat‑treated after fermentation—and acidified cream).

sections.section: 2.4.1 Pre-packaged  liquid  cream is  the  fluid 1   milk  product  obtained  by  preparing  and  packaging  cream, reconstituted cream and/or recombined cream for direct consumption and/or for direct use as such.
sections.summary: Defines 'Pre-packaged liquid cream' as the fluid milk product prepared and packaged from cream, reconstituted cream and/or recombined cream for direct consumption or direct use.

sections.section: 2.4.2 Whipping  cream is  the  fluid 1   cream,  reconstituted  cream  and/or  recombined  cream  that  is  intended  for whipping. When cream is intended for use by the final consumer the cream should have been prepared in a way that facilitates the whipping process.
sections.summary: Defines 'Whipping cream' as fluid cream, reconstituted or recombined cream intended for whipping and, when sold to the final consumer, prepared so as to facilitate the whipping process.

sections.section: 2.4.3 Cream packed under pressure is  the  fluid 1   cream, reconstituted cream and/or recombined cream that is packed with a propellant gas in a pressure-propulsion container and which becomes Whipped Cream when removed from that container.
sections.summary: Defines 'Cream packed under pressure' as fluid cream, reconstituted or recombined cream packed with a propellant gas in a pressure‑propulsion container that becomes whipped cream when dispensed.

sections.section: 2.4.4 Whipped cream is the fluid 1  cream, reconstituted cream and/or recombined cream into which air or inert gas has been incorporated without reversing the fat-in-skimmed milk emulsion.
sections.summary: Defines 'Whipped cream' as cream, reconstituted or recombined cream into which air or an inert gas has been incorporated without reversing the fat‑in‑skimmed milk emulsion.

sections.section: 2.4.5 Fermented cream is the milk product obtained by fermentation of cream, reconstituted cream or recombined cream, by the action of suitable micro-organisms, that results in reduction of pH with or without coagulation. Where the content of (a) specific micro-organism(s) is(are) indicated, directly or indirectly, in the labelling or otherwise  indicated  by  content  claims  in  connection  with  sale,  these  shall  be  present,  viable,  active  and abundant in the product to the date of minimum durability. If the product is heat-treated after fermentation the requirement for viable micro-organisms does not apply.
sections.summary: Defines 'Fermented cream' as cream, reconstituted or recombined cream fermented by suitable micro‑organisms resulting in pH reduction with or without coagulation, and requires any micro‑organisms claimed on the label to be present, viable, active and abundant to the date of minimum durability unless the product is heat‑treated after fermentation, in which case the viability requirement does not apply.

sections.section: 2.4.6 Acidified cream is  the  milk  product obtained by acidifying cream, reconstituted cream and/or recombined cream by the action of acids and/or acidity regulators to achieve a reduction of pH with or without coagulation.
sections.summary: Defines 'Acidified cream' as cream, reconstituted or recombined cream acidified by acids and/or acidity regulators to achieve pH reduction with or without coagulation.

Specifies essential composition and quality factors: permitted raw materials and ingredients for creams and prepared creams, conditions and limits for permitted ingredients (e.g., protein products and milk powders as thickeners up to 20 g/kg; gelatine and starch per GMP), permitted starter cultures and coagulating enzymes for fermented/acidified creams, and a compositional requirement of minimum 10% (w/w) milk fat with non‑compliance affecting use of dairy terms under the General Standard for the Use of Dairy Terms.

Specifies allowable raw materials: milk (which may have undergone mechanical/physical treatments); for reconstituted/recombined creams, butter, milk fat products, milk/cream powders and potable water; for certain prepared creams, concentrated/dried buttermilk; defines 'fluid' as pourable above freezing and cross‑references other Codex standards for ingredient specifications.

Lists permitted ingredients and conditions: only listed ingredients may be used and within specified limits; protein‑based milk/whey products and milk powders may act as stabilizers/thickeners up to 20 g/kg, gelatine and starch may be used per GMP, starter cultures are permitted for fermented cream, and rennet/coagulating enzymes and sodium chloride are permitted for fermented and acidified creams.

Sets composition requirement that milk fat must be at least 10% (w/w) and states that reducing milk fat below this minimum is not compliant with Section 4.3.3 of the General Standard for the Use of Dairy Terms (CXS 206‑1999).

Specifies permitted food additive classes and individual additives with maximum levels or GMP for product categories where their use is technologically justified, provides a product‑category matrix (stabilizers, acidity regulators, thickeners/emulsifiers, packing gases/propellants) and detailed INS listings and limits (e.g., certain phosphates max 1,100 mg/kg expressed as phosphorus; propylene glycol alginate 5,000 mg/kg; permitted packing gases CO2 and N2; propellant N2O).

Requires products covered by the standard to comply with maximum contaminant and toxin levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193‑1995), and requires milk used to comply with those contaminant limits and applicable veterinary drug residue and pesticide MRLs established by the CAC.

Recommends that products be prepared and handled in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1‑1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Milk and Milk Products (CXC 57‑2004) and other relevant Codex texts, and comply with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21‑1997.

Sets labelling requirements in addition to general Codex labelling standards: names must follow Section 2 (with some alternative designations allowed), fat content must be indicated, fermented cream may use traditional terms like 'Acidophilus/Kefir/Kumys' only if fermented with the specified cultures and meeting compositional/microbiological criteria, recombined/reconstituted creams must be labelled if omission would mislead, heat‑treatment description must be provided when omission would mislead, and non‑retail labelling should follow the relevant standard.

Requires the food name to follow Section 2 (pre‑packaged liquid cream may be called 'cream', pressure‑packed cream may use a descriptive term or 'Whipped Cream'), allows alternative national or customary names provided they are not misleading, mandates fat content indication, permits traditional fermented‑milk names only when the specified starter cultures and applicable criteria are met, requires recombined/reconstituted cream to be labelled when omission would mislead, and requires disclosure of heat treatment when its absence would mislead.

Requires declaration of milk fat content acceptable in the country of sale to the final consumer either as a percentage of mass or volume or as grams per serving (with number of servings stated), and allows a numerical fat indication used in the product name to suffice if it includes any additional required information.
States that labelling of non‑retail containers should comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346‑2021).
Requires that compliance with this standard be checked using the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to the provisions of the standard.
